verdicts.question: What type of roof is present at the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 8 material storage photo and page 13 proposed PV area photo both show a flat, dark-tiled/concrete roof surface with no visible slope.
verdicts.rationale: Both the material storage (page 8) and proposed PV area (page 13) photos clearly show a flat RCC roof surface consistent with the designer's answer.

verdicts.question: Are there any obstructions on the roof that could affect the solar installation?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 8 shows the flat roof with only a temporary construction tarpaulin and a neighbouring building's water tank visible in the background (not on this roof). Page 13 shows the proposed PV area is clear of permanent obstructions.
verdicts.rationale: Roof photos on pages 8 and 13 show no permanent obstructions in the proposed PV area; the tarpaulin is clearly temporary construction material.

verdicts.question: What is the angle of the panels? (in degrees)
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 9-10 show SketchUp side-view renders of the MS fabrication structure with panels mounted on elevated posts at a visually low tilt angle, consistent with approximately 10 degrees. The structural design drawings confirm a low-tilt configuration on a flat roof.
verdicts.rationale: SketchUp side-view renders on pages 9-10 show panels at a low tilt consistent with ~10 degrees. The exact angle cannot be measured from the renders alone, but the tilt is clearly not steeper than 15 degrees and matches a typical 10-degree flat-roof MS structure design.

verdicts.question: On how many roofs at the site are we installing solar?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 8 top-view site layout and page 13 proposed PV area show a single continuous flat roof with all panels planned on one roof section. No secondary roof structure is visible.
verdicts.rationale: All photo documentation confirms installation on a single flat RCC roof belonging to one building.

verdicts.question: What type of mounting structure is planned?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 9-12 show SketchUp renders and detailed structural drawings of an elevated MS fabrication structure with vertical columns (100x100 mm), horizontal rails (50x80 mm), diagonal bracing, and panel mounting rails - consistent with a custom MS fabricated elevated frame.
verdicts.rationale: Structural design renders on pages 9-12 confirm an MS fabricated elevated structure with posts and long horizontal rails. The 'Long rails with L clamps' description aligns with how panels attach to the rail system, and the designer's own note clarifies 'MS fabrication structure'.

verdicts.question: What is the orientation of the solar panels?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 8 site layout top-view includes a north compass arrow pointing downward (toward the bottom of the diagram); the panel array fills the upper (south) portion of the roof layout, indicating south-facing orientation. SketchUp renders on page 9 corroborate the tilt direction.
verdicts.rationale: The north compass on the page 8 top-view layout clearly shows north at the lower portion; the panel rows are oriented toward the south side of the roof, consistent with the designer's 'South' answer.

verdicts.question: How can the roof be accessed?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 6-7 show an indoor staircase captioned 'This route will carry the small items.' Page 7 staircase detail shows narrow steps with a metal railing and a 0.3 m width annotation.
verdicts.rationale: Indoor staircase is clearly visible and captioned on pages 6-7, directly confirming staircase access to the roof.

verdicts.question: Is the building currently under construction?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 5 building exterior shows active finishing work: wooden louvre facade being installed at ground level, construction materials on site, and a worker visible. Page 13 shows construction scaffolding/wood below the roof area. Page 14 shows a wooden partition in progress. Page 17 observation notes explicitly state 'wooden partition has to be removed by the customer at the time of installation.' Furthermore, the TSVR's own electrical section notes for Q3 and Q4 state 'Building is under construction.'
verdicts.rationale: The building's structural elements (roof slab, floors) appear complete and the flat roof is accessible; however, active finishing/interior work is clearly ongoing in photos (pages 5, 13, 14) and the TSVR's electrical section notes (Q3, Q4) explicitly read 'Building is under construction,' contradicting the 'No' answer in the roof section. Manual review is needed to determine whether this level of finishing work changes the installation approach.

verdicts.question: In an under-construction building, are existing conduit pipes available for AC, DC cables, and earthing?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 13-14 cable routing photos show a circular conduit pipe running along the exterior wall, but this appears to be the planned solar installation conduit, not a pre-existing building construction conduit. No separate embedded conduit pipes are visible.
verdicts.rationale: Given the uncertain construction status (see Q11 MANUAL_CHECK), and the conduit visible in pages 13-14 appearing to be the planned solar installation run rather than pre-existing construction conduit, this answer cannot be clearly verified. Low confidence due to ambiguity about construction status.

verdicts.question: What is the height of the building and how many floors does it have?
verdicts.designer_answer: Height of the building is 12m, it has G+1+headroom
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 5-6 building exterior photos show: ground floor with wooden facade and entrance, first floor with a balcony and railing, and a raised parapet/headroom structure at the roofline - three distinct levels consistent with G+1+headroom. Exact height of 12 m is not measurable from photos.
verdicts.rationale: Building exterior photos on pages 5-6 clearly show ground floor, one upper floor with balcony, and a headroom/parapet structure at the top - consistent with G+1+headroom. The 12 m height is plausible for a G+1+headroom residential building but cannot be precisely verified from photos alone.

verdicts.question: Is the site photo matching with the building description?
verdicts.photo_observation: Site exterior photos on pages 5-6 show a residential building consistent with the project description (#509, 4th E Cross, HRBR Layout, Kalyan Nagar) with a G+1+headroom structure.
verdicts.rationale: Site photos on pages 5-6 show a residential building matching the described address and building type; the photos are consistent with the building description.

verdicts.question: Is there an overhead HT line passing within a 10-meter radius of the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 5-6 building exterior photos show only low-voltage service/communication cables near the building; no high-tension line towers, HT insulators, or bundled HT cables are visible in the immediate vicinity of the site.
verdicts.rationale: No HT infrastructure (towers, HT insulators, triple-bundle HT cables) is visible in any exterior photo. Only standard LV service wires are present, consistent with the 'No' answer.

verdicts.question: Can the delivery van/truck reach the material unloading point?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 5 material delivery route shows the entrance/doorway area at ground level. Page 6 shows the exterior route for large items (panels). No wide-angle photo of the road or driveway approach for a delivery van is present in the document.
verdicts.rationale: No photo showing road access or driveway approach for a delivery van/truck is included. Material delivery route photos (pages 5-6) show only the internal staircase and exterior lifting path, not the road-to-site approach. Cannot confirm van accessibility from available photos.

verdicts.question: Are there any obstacles at the site that could hinder material lifting?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 7 staircase has a 0.3 m width annotation (narrow for panel movement). Page 14 shows a wooden partition structure in the proposed equipment location area. Page 17 explicitly observes 'wooden partition has to be removed by the customer at the time of installation,' indicating a current obstacle.
verdicts.rationale: Page 17 explicitly flags a wooden partition requiring customer removal before installation, indicating a current obstacle. The narrow staircase (0.3 m, page 7) also poses a practical constraint for material movement. Designer answered 'No' but TSVR's own observations contradict this.

verdicts.question: Where will the Inverter, DCDB, ACDB, Battery, etc. be located?
verdicts.designer_answer: Inverter, DCDB, ACDB, Battery is located near the ground floor.
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 14 shows INVERTER (purple overlay) and BATTERY (green overlay) positioned on a wall at ground floor level inside the building, adjacent to a wooden shelving/partition structure.
verdicts.rationale: Page 14 clearly shows equipment placement overlays for inverter and battery at ground floor level, directly consistent with the designer's answer.

verdicts.question: Is a canopy required for protecting the Inverters/ACDB/DCDB?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 14 shows the proposed inverter and battery location in an indoor/covered ground floor area with wooden shelving/partition above, providing natural shelter from rain.
verdicts.rationale: Page 14 shows equipment location in a covered indoor-like space; natural shelter is available and an external canopy is not required, consistent with the 'No' answer.

verdicts.question: Did you discuss the location of the earth pits with the customer?
verdicts.designer_answer: Earth pits are located near the ground floor (toward south direction)
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 16 earth pit location photo shows DC Earth Pit, AC Earth Pit, and LA Pit clearly marked at the base of the building's exterior south-facing wall, in an open soil/earth area.
verdicts.rationale: Page 16 shows all three pits (DC, AC, LA) marked at ground level in an open soil area at the building's exterior, consistent with the designer's description of ground floor south-direction location.

verdicts.question: Where is the Internet Router located?
verdicts.photo_observation: No dedicated photo of the internet router location is present in the TSVR document.
verdicts.rationale: No photo showing the internet router or its location is available; cannot visually verify.

verdicts.question: Did you pin the site location in your WhatsApp when you were at the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: No screenshot of WhatsApp location pin is present in the TSVR document; this is a procedural step not verifiable from site photos.
verdicts.rationale: WhatsApp location pinning is a procedural/administrative action that cannot be confirmed from site photos.

verdicts.question: What type of energy meter is currently installed?
verdicts.designer_answer: Phase: Three Phase, Type: Digital, If 3-phase: LT w/o CT (5 - 34.999)
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 7 meter box photo shows an open electrical panel with a digital meter at the top (green LED indicator clearly visible) and MCBs/circuit breakers below. The panel size and component layout are consistent with a 3-phase digital meter. No CT coils are visible.
verdicts.rationale: Page 7 shows a digital meter (green LED indicator visible) in the meter panel, consistent with a three-phase digital type. Absence of visible CT coils supports LT w/o CT. Full phase count cannot be definitively confirmed from the photo angle alone.

verdicts.question: Is the Meter cubicle photo matching with the previous answer?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 7 meter box photo shows a digital meter with LED indicator; page 16 shows the same panel with MAIN METER and SOLAR METER areas labeled. Both are consistent with a digital meter setup.
verdicts.rationale: Meter cubicle photos on pages 7 and 16 show a digital meter panel consistent with the three-phase digital meter description, supporting the 'Yes' answer.

verdicts.question: Has the termination point in the Main meter panel been identified?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 16 shows the meter panel with MAIN METER (red overlay) and SOLAR METER (blue overlay) areas labeled; the specific termination/connection point for solar AC cable within the panel is partially obscured by label overlays and not clearly distinguishable in the photo.
verdicts.rationale: Page 16 shows the solar meter space identified in the panel, but the specific solar AC termination point is not clearly visible due to overlay labels covering it. The 'Building is under construction' note adds further uncertainty.

verdicts.question: Is the Termination Point easily visible or sealed?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 16 shows the meter panel open with internal components accessible; the SOLAR METER area is labeled below the main meter, and the panel appears easily openable. The panel is not sealed.
verdicts.rationale: Page 16 shows an open meter panel with visible internal components and labeled solar meter space; the panel is not sealed and the interior is accessible, consistent with the 'Visible' answer.

verdicts.question: Is a Lightning Arrestor (LA) already available at the site?
verdicts.photo_observation: No lightning arrestor equipment (rod, spike, or conductor) is visible in any of the 18 pages. Page 16 (earth pit location) shows only an LA Pit marked for future installation.
verdicts.rationale: No LA hardware is visible anywhere in the site photos. Page 16 confirms only an LA pit location is planned, not an existing LA - consistent with the designer's 'No' answer.

verdicts.question: In Main meter panel, is empty space available for solar meter?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 16 main meter photo shows the panel with MAIN METER labeled at top (red overlay) and a distinct SOLAR METER area labeled below (blue overlay), indicating dedicated available space within the same cubicle.
verdicts.rationale: Page 16 explicitly shows a SOLAR METER space available below the main meter in the same panel cubicle, directly confirming available space.

verdicts.question: In case of elevated structures, is an LA extension provided at the far end?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 9-12 show an elevated MS structure on the flat roof with vertical posts rising approximately 1.4 m above the roof slab. Page 16 shows LA Pit location is planned. However, no photo shows an actual LA extension rod or conductor at the far end of the structure (the structure is not yet installed).
verdicts.rationale: The MS structure is confirmed to be elevated (pages 9-12) and an LA pit is planned (page 16), but no actual or designed LA extension at the far end of the structure is visible in any photo. Cannot confirm the LA extension provision from current documentation.

verdicts.question: Are there any challenges with respect to Installation and Maintenance activities?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 7 staircase has a 0.3 m width annotation (narrow for panel movement). Page 14 shows the equipment placement area has a wooden partition structure in the way. Page 17 observation explicitly notes 'wooden partition has to be removed by the customer at the time of installation.'
verdicts.rationale: Narrow staircase (0.3 m clearance, page 7) and a wooden partition requiring customer removal (page 17 observation) are tangible installation challenges. The designer answered 'No' but the TSVR's own observations suggest otherwise; warrants manual review.

verdicts.question: Are there any safety risks associated with accessing the roof for installation?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 6-7 show staircase access with handrail and narrow-but-functional steps (0.3 m width). The flat roof on pages 8 and 13 is accessible with no visible edge hazards or restricted access. Building is G+1+headroom so roof height is manageable.
verdicts.rationale: Staircase visible on pages 6-7 has a handrail and appears safe for access. The flat roof is accessible and no extreme hazards are visible; consistent with the designer's 'No' answer.

verdicts.question: Is space present in rooftop to store the panels?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 8 material storage photo shows the flat RCC roof with ample open floor area and a tarpaulin with stored material already lying flat on the roof surface.
verdicts.rationale: Page 8 shows the flat roof with adequate open space for horizontal panel storage, directly consistent with the 'horizontally sleeping' answer.

verdicts.question: Is ventilation available in the battery location?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 14 shows the battery placement (BATTERY green overlay) at ground floor near a wooden louvered partition wall with visible slat openings that provide natural air circulation. The area also has an open doorway nearby.
verdicts.rationale: Page 14 shows the battery location adjacent to a wooden louvered partition with visible ventilation slats; natural airflow is available, supporting the 'Yes' ventilation answer.

verdicts.question: Should the Inverters/ACDB/DCDB be installed on a metal grill or wall mount?
verdicts.designer_answer: Inverters/ACDB/D CDB be installed on a wall mounted
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 14 shows the INVERTER overlay positioned flush against a wall surface at ground floor level, indicating wall-mounted placement.
verdicts.rationale: Page 14 shows the inverter positioned against a wall surface, consistent with the wall-mounted installation answer.

verdicts.question: Provide the dimensions for solar meter cubicle placement (L x W of wall).
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 15 shows the solar meter cubicle location marked with a small rectangular overlay on the exterior wall. The space appears compact and consistent with a 400x400 mm cubicle, but no measurement reference is present in the photo to precisely verify the dimensions.
verdicts.rationale: Page 15 shows the solar meter cubicle location on the exterior wall, but actual 400x400 mm dimensions cannot be precisely verified from the overlay photo without measurement references.

verdicts.question: Provide the dimension for earth pits location (L x W of available space).
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 16 earth pit location photo shows DC Earth Pit, AC Earth Pit, and LA Pit marked in an open soil/brick-rubble area at the building base. No measurement scale or reference is present to verify the 500x499 mm available space dimensions.
verdicts.rationale: Page 16 confirms an earth pit location exists in an open soil area, but the specific 500x499 mm available-space dimensions cannot be verified from the photo without measurement references.

verdicts.question: Is LAN cable under customer scope?
verdicts.photo_observation: No photo specifically showing LAN cable routing or scope clarification is present in the TSVR document.
verdicts.rationale: LAN cable scope allocation is a contractual/planning item that cannot be verified from site photos.

verdicts.question: Is there any shadow that affects the solar installation?
verdicts.photo_observation: Page 5 exterior shows a large broad-leafed tree immediately to the left of the building. Page 6 shows a tall palm tree adjacent to the building. Page 8 shows neighbouring buildings at similar height behind the roof. No dedicated shadow analysis photo or shadow diagram is present in the 18-page TSVR.
verdicts.rationale: Trees visible in exterior photos (pages 5-6) are immediately adjacent to the building and could cause partial morning/evening shading on the roof's east/west edges. No shadow analysis diagram is included to confirm 'No shadow.' Cannot confirm designer's answer without a shadow study.

verdicts.question: Is there any trench work to be done to carry the AC cable to the meter location?
verdicts.photo_observation: Pages 13-15 cable routing photos show a circular conduit pipe running entirely above ground along the building's exterior walls from the roof down to ground level; no trench or underground cable run is visible.
verdicts.rationale: Cable routing photos on pages 13-15 clearly show above-ground conduit along the building exterior walls all the way to the meter, confirming no trench work is required.
